clues.clue: Genius billionaire Tony Stark fights crime in a high-tech suit of armor as this hero.
Bitten by a radioactive arachnid, Peter Parker swings through the city as this hero.
This Asgardian god of thunder wields the enchanted hammer Mjolnir.
When Bruce Banner gets angry, he transforms into this enormous green powerhouse.
Blind attorney Matt Murdock protects Hell's Kitchen as this horned vigilante.
The Kellogg brothers created corn flakes in this Michigan 'Cereal City.'
This 'General' company, a Kellogg's rival, makes Cheerios and Lucky Charms.
A famously misleading Post cereal's name promises grapes and these tree seeds, yet contains neither.
Early breakfast cereals were promoted as health food by this Seventh-day religious movement.
Crunchy granola surged in popularity during this hippie counterculture decade.
In this real-estate game, players buy properties like Boardwalk and Park Place.
In this game, players form words from lettered tiles on a 15-by-15 grid.
In this murder-mystery game, suspects include Colonel Mustard and Professor Plum.
This ancient two-player game of kings and pawns ends in checkmate.
In this grid game, you call out coordinates hoping to hear 'you sank my' fleet.
Henry Ford's assembly line cranked out millions of this affordable 'Model.'
Karl Benz patented an early gasoline-powered version of this four-wheeled vehicle in 1886.
This Scottish engineer, namesake of a power unit, vastly improved the steam engine.
Rudolf, whose surname labels a compression-ignition engine and a fuel, pioneered it in the 1890s.
Elisha Otis's safety brake made it safe for skyscrapers to lift people in this vertical car.
This cannibal psychiatrist helped catch Buffalo Bill from behind bars.
This burned dream-stalker wore a bladed glove and haunted Elm Street.
This motel owner, disturbingly devoted to his mother, ran the Bates property.
This 'No Country for Old Men' hitman killed with a captive-bolt cattle gun.
In 'Se7en,' this killer staged murders around the seven deadly sins.

final.clue: These five interlocking colored loops symbolize the union of the world's continents at the Games.
